concepts.title: Gravity
concepts.conceptBody:
Acceleration Due to Gravity

Gravity: If one of the bodies in expression of Newton's law of gravitation is earth than the gravitation is called gravity. Hence gravity is force by which earth attracts a body towards its centre.

When a body is dropped from a certain height above the ground it begins to fall towards the earth under gravity. The acceleration produced in the body due to gravity is called the acceleration due to gravity. It is denoted by g. Its value close to the earth's surface is $9.8\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}/{{\text{s}}^{2}}$.

Suppose that the mass of the earth is $M$, its radius is $R$, then the force of attraction acting on a body of mass $m$ close to the surface of earth is

$\text{F}=\frac{\text{GMm}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}$

According to Newton's second law, the acceleration due to gravity

$\text{g}=\frac{\text{F}}{\text{m}}=\frac{\text{GM}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}$

In vector form,

$\vec{g}=\frac{\text{GM}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}\left( -\overset{}{\mathop{\vec{R}}}\, \right)$

where $\vec{R}$ is position vector from earth's centre to the body of mass m.

This expression is free from $\text{m}$. If two bodies of different masses are allowed to fall freely they will have the same acceleration, i.e., if they are allowed to fall from the same height, they will reach the earth simultaneously.

concepts.title: Variation in Value of 'g' at Height from the Surface
concepts.conceptBody:
Variation in Value of 'g' at Height from the Surface 

Consider earth to be a sphere of mass M, radius $\text{R}$ with centre at $\text{O}$. Let $g$ be the value of acceleration due to gravity at a point A on the surface of earth.

$\therefore \text{ g}=\frac{\text{GM}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}$ … (1)

If ${g}'$ is the acceleration due to gravity at a point $B$, at a height $h$ above the surface of earth, then

$\text{{g}'}=\frac{\text{GM}}{{{(\text{R}+\text{h})}^{2}}}$ … (2)

Dividing (1) by (2), we get

$\frac{{{g}'}}{g} =\frac{GM}{{{(R+h)}^{2}}}\times \frac{{{R}^{2}}}{GM}=\frac{{{R}^{2}}}{{{(R+h)}^{2}}}$ … (3)

$=\frac{{{R}^{2}}}{{{R}^{2}}{{(1+h/R)}^{2}}}={{\left( 1+\frac{h}{R} \right)}^{-2}}$

If $h<<\text{R}$ then $\text{h}/\text{R}$ is very small as compared to 1. Expanding the R.H.S. of above equation by Binomial theorem and neglecting the square and higher powers of $h/R$, we get

$\frac{{\text{{g}'}}}{\text{g}}=1-\frac{2\text{ h}}{\text{R}}$

Or $\text{{g}'}=\text{g}\left( 1-\frac{2\text{ h}}{\text{R}} \right)$ … (4)

So, we note that the value of acceleration due to gravity decreases with height.

It is due to this reason that the value of acceleration due to gravity is lesser at mountains than in plains. At a height equal to the radius of the earth (i.e. $\text{h}=\text{R}=6400\text{ km}$), from (2)

${g}'=\frac{g{{R}^{2}}}{{{(R+R)}^{2}}}=\frac{g}{4}$

Important notes: 

(i) The relation (3) is used to find the value of acceleration due to gravity at a height when $h$ is comparable to the radius of earth $R$ and relation (4) is used to find ${g}'$ when $h$ is very small as compared to $R$.

(ii) With height $\text{h}$, the decrease in the value of $\text{g}$ is $=\text{g}-\text{{g}'}=2 \text{ h}g/\text{R}$

$\therefore$ Fractional decrease in the value of $g$

$=\frac{\text{g}-\text{{g}'}}{\text{g}}=\frac{2 \text{ h}}{\text{R}}$

$\therefore \%$ decrease in the value of $\text{g}$

$=\left( \frac{g-{g}'}{g} \right)\times 100=\frac{2h}{R}\times 100$

concepts.title: Variation in Value of 'g' at Depth from the Surface
concepts.conceptBody:
(b) Variation in value of $g$  at depth from the surface 

Consider earth to be a homogeneous sphere of radius $\text{R}$ and mass $\text{M}$ with center at $\text{O}$. Let $\text{g}$ be the value of acceleration due to gravity at a point $\text{A}$ on the surface of earth, then

$\text{g}=\frac{\text{GM}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}$
If $\rho $ is uniform density of material of the earth, then
$\text{M}$ $~=\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{3}}\rho $
$\therefore $ $\text{ g}$ $=\frac{\text{G}\times \frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{3}}\rho }{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi \text{GR}\rho $ … (5)

Let ${g}'$ be the acceleration due to gravity at the point $B$ at a depth d below the surface of earth. The body at B will experience gravity pull due to shaded portion of earth whose radius is $\left( \text{R}-\text{d} \right)$ and mass is $\text{{M}'}$.

$\therefore \text{ {g}'}=\frac{\text{G}{{\text{M}}^{'}}}{{{(\text{R}-\text{d})}^{2}}}$

And $\text{{M}'}=\frac{4}{3}\pi {{(\text{R}-\text{d})}^{3}}\rho $

$\therefore $ ${g}'$ $=\frac{G\times \frac{4}{3}\pi {{(R-d)}^{3}}\rho }{{{(R-d)}^{2}}}$

$=\frac{4}{3}\pi G\left( R-d \right)\rho $ … (6)

Dividing (6) by (5), we get

$\frac{{\text{{g}'}}}{\text{g}}=\frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi \text{G}\left( \text{R}-\text{d} \right)\rho }{\frac{4}{3}\pi \text{GR}\rho }$

$=\frac{\text{R}-\text{d}}{\text{R}}=\frac{\text{R}}{\text{R}}-\frac{\text{d}}{\text{R}}$

Or ${g}'=g\left( 1-\frac{d}{R} \right)$ … (7)

From (7) we note that the value of acceleration due to gravity decreases with depth.

At the centre of the earth, $d=R,{g}'={{g}_{0}}$ (say).

From (7), we get ${{g}_{0}}=g\left( 1-\frac{R}{R} \right)=0$

It means the acceleration due to gravity is zero at the centre of earth. Therefore the weight of the body of mass $\text{m}$ at the centre of earth $={{\text{m}}_{0}}=0$, but the mass of the body will not be zero.

Thus the value of acceleration due to gravity is maximum at the earth's surface and becomes zero at the centre of the earth.

Note : Decrease in the value of $g$ with depth $d$ is $=g-{g}'=dg/R$

$\therefore $ Fractional decrease in the value of $g$ with

$\text{depth}=\frac{\text{g}-\text{{g}'}}{\text{g}}=\frac{\text{d}}{\text{R}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!%\!\!\text{ }$ decrease in the value of $\text{g}$.

$=\frac{\text{g}-\text{{g}'}}{\text{g}}\times 100=\frac{\text{d}}{\text{R}}\times 100$

concepts.title: Variation in Value of 'g' Due to Non-Uniformity of Earth
concepts.conceptBody:
Variation in Value of 'g' Due to Non-Uniformity of Earth

Earth is not a perfect sphere. It is flattened at the poles and bulges out at the equator. Equatorial radius ${{R}_{e}}$ of the earth is about $21\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ km}$ greater than the polar radius ${{\text{R}}_{\text{p}}}$, Fig.

Now, $\text{g}=\text{GM}/{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

Since G and M are constants

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}\propto 1/{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

Thus we conclude that the value of $g$ is least at the equator and maximum at the pole. It means, the value of acceleration due to gravity increases as we go from equator to the pole.

concepts.title: Variation in Value of 'g' Due to Rotation of Earth
concepts.conceptBody:
Variation in Value of 'g' Due to Rotation of Earth

The earth is rotating about its axis from west to east. So every body on its surface is moving in a circle i.e., is in accelerated motion. So the effective acceleration due to gravity will be

$\text{{g}'}=\text{g}-\text{a}$

where $\text{a}$ is the acceleration of the body towards the centre of earth due to its circular motion.

 

Now, for a body at $\text{P}$ the centripetal acceleration ${{\text{a}}_{\text{r}}}={{\omega }^{2}}\text{r}$ along $\text{POC'}$ $={{\omega }^{2}}R\text{cos }\lambda \left( \text{as }r=R\text{cos }\lambda \right)$; so component of ${{a}_{r}}$ along PO will be $\text{a}={{\text{a}}_{\text{r}}}\text{ cos }\lambda =\text{R }{{\omega }^{2}}\text{cos }\lambda $

Substituting this value of a in the equation ${g}'=g-R{{\omega }^{2}}\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\lambda $ This the required result and from this it is clear that for a given $\omega $, ' $\text{g}$ ' is maximum $\left( =\text{g} \right)$ when $\text{cos}\lambda =\text{min}=0$, i.e. $\lambda =90$ i.e. at the pole and minimum $\left( g-R{{\omega }^{2}} \right)$ when $\text{cos}\lambda =\text{max}=1$ i.e. $\lambda =0$ i.e., at equator. Further more a given point on the surface of the earth $(\lambda =$ const $)\text{g}$ decreases as $\omega $ increases and vice-versa.
